verses.text: In those days there was no king in Israel: and in those days the tribe of the Danites sought them an inheritance to dwell in; for unto that day all their inheritance had not fallen unto them among the tribes of Israel. Guidance: The chapter opens with the refrain ('no king in Israel') and a fact: Dan's inheritance — the territory between Judah and Philistia (Josh 19:40-48) — was never fully taken. The Amorites pushed them up into the hills (Judg 1:34); they failed to drive out their Canaanite neighbors. Now they go looking for an easier land. The verse's quiet diagnosis: the trouble was not God's deed but Dan's faithlessness. The 'inheritance not fallen' is more accurately 'inheritance not taken.' Lazy tribes blame providence.

The children of Dan sent of their family five men from their coasts, men of valour, from Zorah, and from Eshtaol, to spy out the land, and to search it; and they said unto them, Go, search the land: who when they came to mount Ephraim, to the house of Micah, they lodged there. When they were by the house of Micah, they knew the voice of the young man the Levite: and they turned in thither, and said unto him, Who brought thee hither? and what makest thou in this place? and what hast thou here? He said unto them, Thus and thus dealeth Micah with me, and hath hired me, and I am his priest. They said unto him, Ask counsel, we pray thee, of God, that we may know whether our way which we go shall be prosperous. The priest said unto them, Go in peace: before the LORD is your way wherein ye go. Guidance: Five men spy north and recognize the Levite's voice — perhaps they had passed through Bethlehem when he lived there. They ask the freelance priest to consult God. He gives the right blessing-formula ('Go in peace; before the LORD is your way') without actually consulting anyone. A priest with idols on his shelf will say what the customers want to hear. The Danites get the answer they came for, not the one God would have given.

Then the five men departed, and came to Laish, and saw the people that were therein, how they dwelt careless, after the manner of the Zidonians, quiet and secure; and there was no magistrate in the land, that might put them to shame in any thing; and they were far from the Zidonians, and had no business with any man. They came unto their brethren to Zorah and Eshtaol: and their brethren said unto them, What say ye? And they said, Arise, that we may go up against them: for we have seen the land, and, behold, it is very good: and are ye still? be not slothful to go, and to enter to possess the land. When ye go, ye shall come unto a people secure, and to a large land: for God hath given it into your hands; a place where there is no want of any thing that is in the earth. Guidance: Laish is the perfect victim — peaceful, isolated, undefended, with no allies close enough to come to its aid. The spies report it as easy prey. 'For God hath given it into your hands' — the same theological language the conquest generation used at Jericho, but here without God's command. Religious vocabulary appropriated for raids. The chapter's quiet horror is how comfortably the Danites speak of Yahweh while planning what He never told them to do.

There went from thence of the family of the Danites, out of Zorah and out of Eshtaol, six hundred men appointed with weapons of war. They went up, and pitched in Kirjath-jearim, in Judah: wherefore they called that place Mahaneh-dan unto this day: behold, it is behind Kirjath-jearim. They passed thence unto mount Ephraim, and came unto the house of Micah. Guidance: Only six hundred men. The whole northern migration of Dan is the size of a single Philistine garrison. The book has been describing Israel's shrinkage. They camp at Kirjath-jearim (where the ark will later rest twenty years, 1 Sam 7) and name the spot Mahaneh-dan. The same campsite Samson grew up near (Judg 13:25) becomes the launching pad for Dan's idol-stealing expedition. The same geography that once trained a deliverer now trains a thief.

Then answered the five men that went to spy out the country of Laish, and said unto their brethren, Do ye know that there is in these houses an ephod, and teraphim, and a graven image, and a molten image? now therefore consider what ye have to do. They turned thitherward, and came to the house of the young man the Levite, even unto the house of Micah, and saluted him. The six hundred men appointed with their weapons of war, which were of the children of Dan, stood by the entering of the gate. The five men that went to spy out the land went up, and came in thither, and took the graven image, and the ephod, and the teraphim, and the molten image: and the priest stood in the entering of the gate with the six hundred men that were appointed with weapons of war. Guidance: The spies do not forget what they saw on the way out. The chapter's irony: a tribe migrating because it failed to take the inheritance God gave it, now stealing icons it has no right to. Six hundred armed men at the gate, the five spies in the back room raiding the shrine. The Levite stands at the entryway with the soldiers — already standing on the wrong side of the door. He is about to be promoted out of Micah's chapel into Dan's national shrine. Compromise has an upward ladder; the next rung is always available.

These went into Micah's house, and fetched the carved image, the ephod, and the teraphim, and the molten image. Then said the priest unto them, What do ye? And they said unto him, Hold thy peace, lay thine hand upon thy mouth, and go with us, and be to us a father and a priest: is it better for thee to be a priest unto the house of one man, or that thou be a priest unto a tribe and a family in Israel? And the priest's heart was glad, and he took the ephod, and the teraphim, and the graven image, and went in the midst of the people. Guidance: 'Hold thy peace, lay thine hand upon thy mouth' — be quiet, do not protest. Then the bribe in the form of a question: would you rather serve one household or one whole tribe? The pitch is upward mobility for a religious career. 'The priest's heart was glad.' One sentence — and the spiritual portrait is complete. He carries his stolen idols 'in the midst of the people.' Religion that follows the biggest paycheck is religion that becomes the official theology of whoever pays best. He does not even argue about the theft. The same hands that 'consecrated' him under Micah now lift the idols for Dan.

So they turned and departed, and put the little ones and the cattle and the carriage before them. When they were a good way from the house of Micah, the men that were in the houses near to Micah's house were gathered together, and overtook the children of Dan. They cried unto the children of Dan. They turned their faces, and said unto Micah, What aileth thee, that thou comest with such a company? He said, Ye have taken away my gods which I made, and the priest, and ye are gone away: and what have I more? and what is this that ye say unto me, What aileth thee? The children of Dan said unto him, Let not thy voice be heard among us, lest angry fellows run upon thee, and thou lose thy life, with the lives of thy household. The children of Dan went their way: when Micah saw that they were too strong for him, he turned and went back unto his house. Guidance: Micah's pursuit gets the chapter's most painfully accurate line: 'Ye have taken away my gods which I made.' The phrase is a self-refutation — gods you can make are gods that can be stolen. The Danites' reply is pure power: shut up or our angry men will kill you and your family. Might-makes-right is the chapter's actual theology, beneath all the priestly vocabulary. Micah, helpless, turns home. He has lost the silver, the shrine, the priest, and any illusion that he could buy God's favor. The chapter has no pity for him; idolatry's investments do not insure against theft.

They took the things which Micah had made, and the priest which he had, and came unto Laish, unto a people that were at quiet and secure: and they smote them with the edge of the sword, and burnt the city with fire. There was no deliverer, because it was far from Zidon, and they had no business with any man; and it was in the valley that lieth by Beth-rehob. They built a city, and dwelt therein. And they called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father, who was born unto Israel: howbeit the name of the city was Laish at the first. Guidance: They massacre a peaceful village. The narrator names Laish's defenselessness ('no deliverer, far from Zidon, had no business with any man') without spinning it. This is conquest by opportunity, not by command. They build a city, rename it Dan after their tribal founder, and the famous border 'from Dan to Beersheba' is finally complete — but with a stolen idol on the altar at one end. The northern boundary of Israel will house the kingdom's first idolatrous shrine; centuries later, Jeroboam will put one of his golden calves here (1 Kings 12:29). The book of Judges is writing the seed that grows into the divided kingdom's theology.

They took the things which Micah had made, and the priest which he had, and came unto Laish, unto a people that were at quiet and secure: and they smote them with the edge of the sword, and burnt the city with fire. There was no deliverer, because it was far from Zidon, and they had no business with any man; and it was in the valley that lieth by Beth-rehob. They built a city, and dwelt therein. And they called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father, who was born unto Israel: howbeit the name of the city was Laish at the first. Guidance: They massacre a peaceful village. The narrator names Laish's defenselessness ('no deliverer, far from Zidon, had no business with any man') without spinning it. This is conquest by opportunity, not by command. They build a city, rename it Dan after their tribal founder, and the famous border 'from Dan to Beersheba' is finally complete — but with a stolen idol on the altar at one end. The northern boundary of Israel will house the kingdom's first idolatrous shrine; centuries later, Jeroboam will put one of his golden calves here (1 Kings 12:29). The book of Judges is writing the seed that grows into the divided kingdom's theology.

They took the things which Micah had made, and the priest which he had, and came unto Laish, unto a people that were at quiet and secure: and they smote them with the edge of the sword, and burnt the city with fire. There was no deliverer, because it was far from Zidon, and they had no business with any man; and it was in the valley that lieth by Beth-rehob. They built a city, and dwelt therein. And they called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father, who was born unto Israel: howbeit the name of the city was Laish at the first. Guidance: They massacre a peaceful village. The narrator names Laish's defenselessness ('no deliverer, far from Zidon, had no business with any man') without spinning it. This is conquest by opportunity, not by command. They build a city, rename it Dan after their tribal founder, and the famous border 'from Dan to Beersheba' is finally complete — but with a stolen idol on the altar at one end. The northern boundary of Israel will house the kingdom's first idolatrous shrine; centuries later, Jeroboam will put one of his golden calves here (1 Kings 12:29). The book of Judges is writing the seed that grows into the divided kingdom's theology.

The children of Dan set up the graven image: and Jonathan, the son of Gershom, the son of Manasseh, he and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan until the day of the captivity of the land. They set them up Micah's graven image, which he made, all the time that the house of God was in Shiloh. Guidance: The Levite's name is finally given: Jonathan, grandson of Gershom — and Gershom was Moses' son (Exod 2:22). The Hebrew text inserts a letter into 'Moses' to make it read 'Manasseh' (the scribes were so embarrassed that Moses' own grandson started Israel's longest idolatrous line that they edited the consonants); the original is almost certainly 'Moses.' The grandson of the lawgiver becomes the founding priest of the kingdom's first idol-shrine. The same family that brought the law down from Sinai produces the priests of the calf-cult that lasts 'until the day of the captivity of the land' — six hundred years, all while 'the house of God was in Shiloh.' Two shrines existed in parallel: the legitimate one in Shiloh, the illegitimate one at Dan. Most of the kingdom worshiped at the wrong one. The chapter does not preach; it just records the address of the long disaster.
